channel.title: Electric Guitar Build 2025-2026 on DAEBFCG
channel.description: Recent content in Electric Guitar Build 2025-2026 on DAEBFCG

item.title: Bridge Mounting
item.description:
We will be installing 6 of these.

Determine Intonation Points

The first thing to do is determine the lengthwise positions for the bridges. This will be done for the two outermost strings, and the inner four strings will be put in a straight connecting line between them. I am not going to directly use the measured scale lengths (25.5" low E, and 25" high E), but instead determine the positions empirically so as to include the additional length needed to account for string stretching, etc. (i.e. “compensation”). The bridge saddles are adjustable for fine tuning the intonation, which is good, but I would like to try to get them close so minimal adjustment is needed.

I set up an arrangement where the two outer e strings are attached to tuners, with a temporary nut and frets at the 0 and 12 position. The bottom end of the strings are attached to a jig I made for this operation that acts as a tailpiece to hold the strings. The idea is that I can compare the 12th fret harmonic with the 12th fret fretted note and move the bridge forward or backward until the pitches are equal. That will give the the intonation point for the string, and thus determine where the bridge should be mounted.

Once this is done for the high and low e strings, a line is drawn from point to point, which should make about a 13-14 degree angle.

I first tried to use the actual bridges, but the tail end of them is too high to fit right.

I switched to a small triangular block of wood, which works much better, and is easier to mark the intonation point.

First Attempt

My first method for this was to get the two outermost bridges screwed in, and then lay out the remaining four in a line and screw them in. Unfortunately, the planned distance between the outermost strings on this guitar (2.0625") is a bit more than the minimal width these bridges are designed for so there will be some gap between them. So I cannot just lay them in side to side touching each other.

The ideal side to side placement in this case is much like the principle used for string spacing on a nut - the thicker strings get a slightly larger gap between them, so overall the strings come out equally spaced.

I tried calculating the positions taking into account this gap, but it worked out to about 1/100th of an inch extra between the low e, a, and d strings, and 0 gap betwen the treble strings.

I took a shot at this, basically eyeballing the gaps and drilled the 3/32 holes for the screws.

Here is how that worked out.

It is not terrible, but far from perfect. The worst part is that the d string bridge is too low, which throws off the line and sort of stands out as not right. Also the gaps are not nearly what I had hoped.

This is all a result of the way I drilled the screw holes.

Looking at the holes, it is clear there is some misalignment.

I decided that it is not acceptable, and will need to try again.

ReDo

This time I worked out exactly where all the holes should go on the computer and printed out a template. The template has cross-lines whereever a hole should go, and I used a fine pointed scratch awl to pinprick those locations through the paper and into the wood.

First I have to fill in the holes from the first attempt, though. I made 6 little 3/32 dowels from cherry and CA glued them in, and cut flush.

This technique of twisting some wood through a hole in metal works amazingly well for making small dowels.

The holes are nicely plugged now, giving a clean slate for another try. This will all be covered by the bridges, so no one will ever see these. (Fortunately I only drilled the top hole for each bridge.)

Template

Here is the template.

Ideally, this would be 3D printed or made by CNC with the holes premade.

A test placement looks good.

The next improvement to my process was to start by drilling very small pilot holes, like 3/64 inch, then working up to 5/64 and then 3/32 incrementally, instead of going right in with the 3/32 bit.

First impressions:

The alignment looks much better. The gapping is still not perfect.

This will have to be good enough.

(Also visible are some remnants of wax I used on the screws to make them easier to screw in.)

String Holes

Now for the string holes. These bridges can support either top-load or string-through-body, so I am going to set up for both options. Again using a pin prick awl, I made the holes along a the line from the outermost strings, and drilled the 1/8 holes with a brad point bit.

Then used a 5/16 brad point to make the recess for the top-loading option.

The 1/8 holes need to go all the way through the body for the string-through-body option. For that I used the pin trick to get the drill bit exactly aligned from the top hole so I could complete the hole from the back.

String through holes from the back.

Some metal string ferrules will be mounted in the back of the guitar where these 1/8 holes are now. The ferrules have not arrived yet, so I will wait to drill out the recesses for those.

These look to be in good alignment.

item.title: Fretboard Radius
item.description:
The guitar is positioned and supported so that the neck remains flat and does not flex during the sanding process. Some protective paper is used to cover the body and headstock.

The neck support block used here is a rolling-pin holder I picked up from a flea market for $3. StewMac sells the same thing, lined with cork, for over $20.

A StewMac 12 inch radius sanding block is used. (A longer radius beam would be ideal.) A section of 80 grit sanding-belt is stuck to the concave surface with double-sided tape.

The important thing is to ensure that the fretboard surface remains flat and of equal thickness all around during sanding.

This is followed up with 220 grit. I will take another few passes with 400-grit before installing the frets.

The fretboard now has a nice 12 inch radius across the top.

Fortunately any overly deep dot inlays have now come to the surface, and the slotting-mishap scratch at the 20th fret has completely disappeared.

The side dots are also centered now, so my calculations were correct in placing them slightly below center.

A knotty spot around the 8th and 9th frets has been revealed, and the wood there seems hard yet more brittle than typical walnut. I think this will be ok, and hopefully made durable with the application of Tung oil. I am a little worried though. Will the guitar need a fretboard replacement someday?

item.title: Neck Carving
item.description:
The general procedure is simple - remove everything that does not look like a neck.

These photos are slightly out of order and organized roughly into distinct steps, but in reality the whole neck process is one fluid activity with lots of iteration.

Holding Jig

A special cruciform holding jig that can be firmly held in the vise is made to support the guitar for neck carving.

Some additional 1/4 mdf and padding is added to the back to prop up the body since the fretboard is already attached.

The guitar firmly held face down to the cruciform with three cam clamps.

Taper

The sides of the (currently square) neck are tapered to match the fretboard using a block plane.

On a neck that is being made unattached to the body, the bandsaw is better suited. In this case, the headstock and body are in the way.

Thickness

The target final thickness of the neck is approximately 3/4 inch. It is currently 1 - 1.5 inch thick (since it extends into the body), so a significant amount of wood will need to be removed.

I made some cross cuts to the depth and sawed the out the excess with the Ryoba saw, then cleaned up with a chisel.

To be sure I did not cut too deep, I set up a guide rail using some aluminum angle bar. For future reference, leave an additional 1/8 inch thickness.

This is also usually done on the bandsaw, but not feasible with the body attached.

Heel

To start the neck shaping I will focus on the neck-to-body transition (i.e. the heel) first.

The plan is to create a smooth transition under the fretting hand, with the curve based on the multi-scale fret angles.

Headstock Transition

Next the neck-to-headstock transition is worked on.

Shaping

With the headstock and heel areas roughed out, the overall shaping can be done.

The first tool using is the Shinto rasp, which removes a lot of wood quickly get the initial facets cut.

This is followed up with a four-in-hand rasp to further whittle the neck into the rounded shape.

The edges where it meets the fretboard are cut down with a chisel.

The thickness is checked along the way.

Starting to look like a neck.

Heel Refinement

With the neck closer to its final shape, the heel shape is refined.

A test fit with the fretting hand is done to be sure it feels right.

The body cutaway curves are also refined into their final contour and roundover.

Headstock Refinement

The same refinement is also done at the headstock end.

Now the fretboard-to-headstock transition is cleaned up.

Sanding

To finish up, the entire neck is sanded with 80 and 100 grit paper to remove all rasp marks and perform the last bit of shaping work.

Finally, it is sanded all over with 150 grit to remove any scratches.

The cherry wood has some odd dark spots and pitch-pockets. The crescent shape near the headstock is not a carving mistake, but a dark spot in the wood.

item.title: Fretboard Glue Down (The Right Way)
item.description:
First a new spruce filler cap over the truss rod is glued in, and then planed down flush.

Fretboard and neck ready to glue again, with new alignment dowels.

This time around I used a traditional fretboard clamping caul, which is made from plywood and particleboard with a cork backing.

The advantage of this method is that the clamps are placed carefully and tightened in an even and controlled way.

I suspect that my previous failed attempts were due to uneven clamping pressure exerting a pulling force to one side which caused the slippage.

A possible drawback of using this type of caul is that it may bend slightly under clamping pressure and not end up perfectly flat. Next time I might use a small slab of stone or something like that as the caul, which would be be ideal. I think this may not necessarily be crucial since I will be sanding the fretboard to a radius shape and leveling it in the process.

Results

From this angle, the fretboard center-line is dead on with the body center.

Looking down the body and neck to check for alignment, things look pretty good.

Overall a satisfactory result, although maybe not 100% perfect. This is the first time I have glued a fretboard to a guitar with the neck and body already attached and it does required more alignment precision than when gluing to a separate neck.

The last glue joint to be made on this guitar is now done.

Remaining Steps

(Approximate order of operations)

  1. Carve the neck to shape
  2. Clean up fretboard headstock/body transitions
  3. Radius-sand the fretboard
  4. Drill holes for bridges and string-through ferrules
  5. Sand down to finish-grit and apply first wash-coat of shellac
  6. Install frets
  7. Treat fretboard with tung-oil
  8. Level and crown frets
  9. Install tuners and hardware
  10. Do basic setup
  11. Install pickups
  12. Experiment with electronics circuit designs
  13. Make the truss rod cover plate
  14. Make the control cavity cover plate
  15. Drill holes for pots and switches
  16. Solder together electronics components and test
  17. Remove all removable parts and apply final French-Polish finish
  18. Put all back together and do final setup

ETA: 6-8 weeks

item.title: Fretboard Removal
item.description:
Here is where we landed with the failed glue-down. It is misaligned by about 1/16 inch at the body end. I have decided that it must be removed and reattached. Good thing I used hide glue.

Also good is that most of the wood shown here is going to be carved or sanded away eventually, so any injuries during the removal process are not a likely major cause of concern.

Step 1 is to protect the body and headstock areas with some heavy masking paper.

Ungluing Tools

  1. Wallpaper steamer
  2. Heat gun
  3. Putty knife (with corners filed round)
  4. Cake frosting spatula

Sometimes just the heat gun and spatula work. For bigger jobs like this that require more time and heat, some steam is good to keep things flexible. The steam from this Wagner power steamer really penetrates and gets the glue moving again.

Process

The first thing to look for is a corner where the putty knife or spatula can get hold.

I start with the heat gun to heat things up overall. (Note use of aluminum foil to protect wood from heat gun.) I ended up just using the steamer and putty knife for the remainder of this job.

Gradually it lifts up.

And it is off! It looks like a big mess, but no harm done.

Clean up

The fretboard got rather bent. While still hot and wet, I cleaned off as much glue as I could and clamped it flat to cool and dry.

The neck cleans cleans up quite well, too. The thin fillet of wood covering the truss rod got destroyed, though, but I can redo that easily enough.

Next up: fretboard glue down take-4.

item.title: Fretboard Glue Down (The Wrong Way)
item.description:
With the truss rod installed the fretboard can now be glued. The location dowels are locking the fretboard in place and should prevent any slip. Or so I thought.

This is my first time trying this clamping approach. Once the fretboard is set into place and the glue grabs, it is flipped face down on to a rigid and flat surface to act as a clamping caul. A strip of cork is placed between the fretboard and the rigid surface to protect and account for any deviations in pressure or flatness. The thinking is that the fretboard and neck should be glued into as flat a position as possible to minimize future movement and need for much fret-leveling.

This huge slab of soapstone is dead flat and seems like the perfect thing.

Then it is just a matter of clamping hard and letting dry for 24 hours.

Fails 1 and 2

The first two attempts resulted in the fretboard slipping off center so were aborted early. I deduced that the 3/16 poplar dowels being used for positioning pins were getting soft from the glue and losing their holding power.

Fail 3

I added two more position dowels, this time using 1/4 inch oak dowels. I also remade the 3/16 poplar dowel pins - four total position pins. The fretboard should NOT move now and seems well locked when fitted dry.

This slipped too. I think it is the clamping.

  • Too much force with the monster C Clamps.
  • The belly carve in the back of the guitar is opposite the body-end of the fretboard so must be clamped there as well, and the curve is causing the clamp to pull to the side.

Unfortunately I did not notice this until too late so I decided to just let it stay put and see how bad it turned out the next day.

Results

The good news is that the soapstone slab did keep the fretboard very flat and made a nice tight joint.

The bad news is that it did indeed slip off center.

The slip is mostly prominent at the body end. It is off there about 1/16-3/32 inches.

See how bad it is off the intended line here.

Looking down the center, it is fairly noticeable that the neck is not straight with the body. The body end of the fretboard is just slightly too far to the right.

This is slight and can possibly be compensated for in a couple ways.

  1. Move the bridge location slightly off center, or
  2. Keep the bridge in the center and let the strings land where they may, letting an outer string (in this case the bass side) veer off the fretboard as it moves away from the nut.

Since the center lines of this guitar are so integral to the design, moving the bridge to one side will look unacceptably bad, as this experiment with a stand-in bridge shows.

I would probably try to finagle this on a guitar where the center line is not so prominent, and just deal with it.

With option 2 the low e string will likely not even be playable. Also, the fretboard dots would not be between the two middle strings, which looks ugly. Furthermore the strings may not easily line up with the pickup pole pieces.

This option is not going work either, as it would highlight the misalignment.

Decision

Remove the fretboard and try again. This option is only viable since I used hide glue.

Next time I will use my proven clamping technique with a wood caul placed on top. It will not have the advantage of being held perfectly flat, but will be easier to see if things are going awry.

item.title: Truss Rod Installation
item.description:
Before installing the truss rod I would like to cover up the bottom of this drill hole to make it look nicer when someone opens the cover to the adjustment access port.

A round piece of maple veneer works nicely. I like to add a little note to whoever will be doing the adjustment and let them know for sure what size allen wrench to use.

Preview of how it will appear with the fretboard installed and the adjustment cover removed. (The truss rod adjustment cover plate will be fabricated later.)

Setting the truss rod

The truss rod fits in snugly, but I am opting to put a few dabs of silicone caulk under it to prevent any rattles. One dab on each end and one in the middle. It is imperative to not get any silicone on the gluing surface, hence the masking tape. I have already sealed the truss rod channel with shellac so am not worried about the silicone seeping through into the neck wood. Silicone must never get in or on bare wood. I hardly ever touch the stuff.

Once installed, it is clamped down tightly and left overnight to let the silicone fully cure.

The truss rod is in for good.

It is a little deep, and has left a very slight gap between where it will hit against the bottom of the fretboard. Not show, but I glued in a thin fillet of spruce to fill the gap.

item.title: Jack Mount
item.description:
The proposed location of the jack and the angle into the control cavity, done by eye.

First a shallow 1 inch round hole to create the recess for the lip is drilled with a forstener bit.

Then the remaining 7/8 hole is drilled straight through to the control cavity.

Test fit. A little deep, but depth was hard to get perfect on the rounded body.

The pilot holes for the screw were drilled with a hand crank drill. I hit the body in one place with the drill chuck causing a small dent, visible just below the jack mount.

The dent can be steamed out by wetting it and hitting it with the heat gun until the dented wood swells back out.

Dent all gone!

Some minor chip out around the bottom will require the edges of the jack mount hole to be sanded smooth. That is unfortunate as I would prefer a sharp edge. But looks acceptable.

item.title: Body Sculpting
item.description:
I started out by applying a wash coat of shallac around the edges to stiffen up the wood fibers in preparation for the routing.

I used a 1/4 inch roundover bit over the top and back (but not going all the way to the ends of the horns). I was worried the curly maple might chip or tear out, but it was fine. A nice, clean, round cut was made.

I went back over it by hand to smooth it out. I am keeping the ends of the horns sharp, so the rounded edge fades into the tips.

After rounding over the back edges the same way, I used a random-orbit sander with a 60-git stripping pad to sculpt out a slight belly to the back surface where the guitar meets the body of the player. This was done partially to compensate for a low area in one of the cherry boards used for the neck-core. It was about 1/8 inch too shallow. So I scooped out the back a bit giving it a nice smooth contour. This does have a nice feel to it when held against the ribs in the playing position. You can see here that the back dips in slightly around the waist area.

I went over the back edges again, and hand faded the roundover into the sharp horn ends. The fade from round to sharp feels and looks just as I had hoped. Once the neck is carved some final adjustments to the body contour around the cutaways and horns will be made, but the major work to the body is now done.

Here is the back with another temporary coat of shellac.

Quite pleased!

item.title: Post Routing Repair 1
item.description:
This small triangle of the cherry wood broke out during routing. It was probably caused by an existing hairline crack in the end of the board.

I found a small matching piece of the cherry wood used for the body that fits into the corner pretty well. This is glued and secured tightly with tape overnight.

Then cut with a flush cut saw fairly close.

And then carefully chiseled down flush with the body.

Barely visible, except for one corner that chipped out. I will need to fill this with sawdust/glue putty. There are a few other very minor holes (bug or worm holes) in the cherry that have appeared which will also be filled prior to finishing.
